claim: A working web-design engineer running a 6,000+ note vault uses a numbered PARA+Zettelkasten hybrid (00 MOCs, 01 Projects, 02 Areas, 03 Resources, 04 Permanent, 05 Fleeting, 06 Daily, 07 Archives, 99 Meta) and reports that adding folder structure made AI tooling and human navigation dramatically easier.
quote: This makes it much easier for Claude Code to work on notes, and it also helps humans find things more easily.

claim: PARA+Zettelkasten fusion typically applies PARA at the top level for projects/areas and pushes atomic, bidirectionally-linked permanent notes into Areas, with the explicit warning to cherry-pick rather than copy whole systems.
quote: cherry-pick the most applicable and interesting ideas

claim: Nick Milo's LYT framework has consolidated from the six-folder ACCESS scheme into the three-folder ACE (Atlas / Calendar / Efforts), framed around three head-spaces — knowledge, time, action — with the principle that structure must be earned.
quote: ACE is really about the head spaces we orient our thoughts around.

claim: The original ACCESS structure (Atlas, Calendar, Cards, Extras, Sources, Spaces) was always positioned by its author as a starting template to be torn apart, not a prescription.
quote: The ACCESS folder structure stands for: • Atlas •Calendar •Cards •Extras •Sources •Spaces It's not the gospel. It's meant for you to tear apart and make your own.

claim: A 2026 technical user pairs Johnny Decimal (numeric IDs like 61.14) with Obsidian Bases inside an ALPPS top-level layout (Archives, Log, People, Projects, Spaces), arguing the friction of assigning IDs is a feature that forces intentional taxonomy.
quote: The JD IDs can look ridiculous at times. '61.14 Bill' is less pretty than just 'Bill.' And once you assign an ID, moving things around is annoying.

claim: Forum users running JD inside Obsidian agree the index and notes must live in the same vault, but flag scaling pain once a category accumulates 100+ granular IDs and confusion when conflated with Zettelkasten.
quote: when u have 100 granular id for each travel, events, entertainment...Doesnt that make it hard to find information?

claim: Obsidian's CEO keeps almost all notes in the vault root and replaces folder hierarchy with a categories property surfaced through Bases, deliberately avoiding folders because notes belong to multiple areas of thought.
quote: I avoid folders because many of my entries belong to more than one area of thought.

claim: The Jellyfish vault pattern dumps every note into one folder and relies on YAML properties (context, type, status, topic) plus Dataview dashboards for organisation, eliminating folder-placement decisions entirely.
quote: All notes go into one folder, known as the 'Jellyfish'.

claim: The current 2026 consensus on a technical vault is a three-layer hybrid: shallow folders (max two deep) for high-level separation, MOCs for navigation, tags for cross-cutting filtering — with tags treated as signals rather than structure.
quote: Tags are signals, not structure

claim: Developers on the Obsidian forum report that pure Zettelkasten 'does not adjust very good when writing technical documents' and instead organise by content type — snippets, tools/libraries, running logs, conceptual notes — often in a topical 'notebooks' folder beside a separate Zettelkasten folder.
quote: does not adjust very good when writing technical documents

claim: A three-year-tested opinionated workflow rejects MOCs altogether, organises folders by file type, and makes nested-tag search the primary discovery mechanism, arguing nested tags express hierarchy that links cannot.
quote: tag searches (not links, viewing backlinks, etc.) are my primary mode of 'discovery.'

claim: A widely-shared 2026 reset reduces the vault to five buckets — Inbox, System, Active, Vault (evergreen), Recipes — and replaces nested folders with a Capture > Tag > Backlink workflow using status tags like #status/seed and #status/growth.
quote: Personal > Writing > 2026 > Blog Posts > Tech > Drafts > File

claim: A Zettelkasten practitioner with 6,000+ notes reports that Obsidian's graph view becomes unusable at that scale even on an M4 Mac mini, forcing external tools like Cosmograph for actual network exploration.
quote: The layout becomes sluggish even in a Mac mini M4, every movement lags, and eventually the app freezes.

claim: Obsidian Properties are typed YAML metadata stored at the top of notes; supported types are text, list, number, checkbox, date, and date & time, with tags as a special list-typed property.
quote: Properties have types (e.g. text, list, date, number, checkbox) making data entry faster and more accurate. Tags is a special property type exclusively assigned to the tags property.

claim: A given property name can only carry one type across the whole vault, which is what makes consistent filtering and querying possible.
quote: A property name can only have one type across the entire vault. Once a property is assigned a type (e.g., status as Text), all notes using status must use the Text type.

claim: Obsidian 1.9.0 (May 2025) shipped Bases as a core plugin that turns notes into database-like table views driven by YAML properties, introducing a new .base file format.
quote: Bases lets you create custom table views to visualize and interact with data in your vault. This is an early beta. We expect many changes and improvements to Bases over the coming months.

claim: Bases queries the same seven Properties types plus implicit fields like file path/folder, supports three-component filters (property/operator/value) with and/or/not, and currently only renders table views; inline Dataview-style properties are not supported.
quote: The plugin works with seven property types: Text, List, Number, Checkbox, Date, and Date & Time. It also supports implicit properties, like file path or folder. Inline properties aren't supported.

claim: Dataview reads YAML frontmatter and inline `key:: value` fields equivalently, so authors can mix both; inline fields use double colons and can sit in lists, tasks, or sentences via [bracket:: value] syntax.
quote: Inline fields use a double colon :: between key and value, while YAML frontmatter fields use a single colon. You can embed metadata inside sentences or multiple fields on the same line using bracket syntax like [rating:: 9].

claim: Practitioners are migrating off Dataview inline fields onto YAML frontmatter because Dataview is largely unmaintained and heavy queries cause keystroke lag, with Bases and Datacore positioned as the longer-term query layer; Meta Bind is used to keep YAML values editable inline.
quote: lag in keystrokes when typing into pages with heavy Dataview queries... Dataview treats inline fields and YAML frontmatter the same. The Meta Bind plugin became essential, allowing fields stored in YAML to display inline.

claim: A maintainable Bases-era vault depends on a central master property schema and a mandatory `type` property on every queryable note, which acts as the primary discriminator each Base filters on.
quote: Establishing a consistent, vault-wide Universal Data Language through a master property schema is therefore not optional... The cornerstone of a schema is a mandatory type property for every note intended for use with Bases.

claim: As of Obsidian 1.9 the singular keys `tag`, `alias`, and `cssclass` are deprecated in favour of plural list forms `tags`, `aliases`, `cssclasses`, and ISO date/datetime formats are recommended for reliable sorting.
quote: As of Obsidian 1.9, the old singular properties tag, alias, and cssclass are deprecated. Use the plural forms and make them lists. Prefer ISO format (YYYY-MM-DD) and ISO datetime (YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM) for reliable sorting.

claim: Obsidian autocompletes property names from existing vault keys when you type, which is the cheapest mechanism for keeping a schema consistent and avoiding duplicate keys (status vs progress, due_date vs deadline).
quote: Obsidian automatically keeps track of every property you use across your vault. When you go to add a property to a new note, it will suggest existing property keys, helping you avoid typos and duplicates.

claim: In 1.9.1 Obsidian renamed Bases built-in functions from snake_case to camelCase to align with the Obsidian API surface, an early sign that Bases formulas are stabilising as a public schema.
quote: renamed the built-in functions for Bases from snake_case to camelCase, a naming schema considered more consistent with the Obsidian API.

claim: Obsidian Web Clipper is the official, free, open-source browser extension that saves articles, highlights, and selections as portable Markdown files with template-driven metadata extraction across Chrome, Safari, Firefox, Edge, Brave, Arc, Orion, and Vivaldi.
quote: Highlight and capture the web in your favorite browser... All content stored locally in your Obsidian vault. Clips saved as Markdown files you control.

claim: Reviewers in 2026 frame the Obsidian Web Clipper as a free alternative to Readwise that wins on deep vault integration and template customization, while Readwise Reader still wins for heavy in-line annotation flows.
quote: If you are building a long-term knowledge library and want deep Obsidian integration with rich template customization, the native Obsidian Web Clipper is the right foundation. If you annotate extensively as you read, Readwise Reader adds a layer that the native clipper cannot match.

claim: The official Readwise Obsidian plugin auto-syncs highlights from Kindle, Apple Books, Instapaper, Pocket, Twitter, PDFs, etc. into per-source notes, append-only so nothing in the vault is overwritten, with Jinja2 templates for metadata.
quote: If the highlight is from a new book, article, or Twitter account, a new page in the Obsidian vault will be made; if the highlight is from an existing source, the highlight will be appended to the bottom of the existing page.

claim: The community Obsidian Hypothesis plugin syncs web article highlights and annotations from Hypothes.is into the vault on startup or manual trigger, updating existing articles and supporting Nunjucks templates.
quote: An Obsidian.md plugin that syncs highlights from Hypothesis... sync web article highlights/annotations on Obsidian startup or manual trigger, update existing articles with new highlights and annotations.

claim: A widely shared Mac/iOS pattern combines Advanced URI, Templater, and Hotkeys for Templates so a global hotkey or iOS Shortcut creates a dated note, then Opt+A/D/M archive, trash, or move it during inbox triage; the next note auto-opens.
quote: creates a new dated note with contents entered on the go... automatically opens the next note in the folder after the previous note has been processed.

claim: QuickAdd is the dominant capture-template plugin in 2026 — a single command appends a timestamped bullet to today's daily note (creating it if missing), with macros chaining template + capture + script for richer flows.
quote: Capture allows you to quickly add content to predefined files... Daily Notes/{{DATE:gggg-MM-DD}}.md... appends a timestamped bullet to today's note.

claim: The Inbox Organiser plugin captures every new note into an inbox folder and periodically prompts the user to file each into a target folder, encoding the 'capture first, organize later' principle as a recurring nag.
quote: Obsidian plugin to capture any new notes into an inbox and periodically prompt to organise these into other folders within the vault.

claim: Community PKM consensus is that an Inbox folder is the single point of entry for mobile/quick captures and unprocessed notes, with QuickAdd + Templater applying a quick-note template at capture time and a weekly review pass for triage.
quote: The Inbox folder is one of the most important folders... where temp notes, quick notes, or notes captured on mobile are stored.

claim: Mobile capture is the most common pain point on r/ObsidianMD; Obsidian has mobile quick capture on its official roadmap but most users currently rely on third-party companion apps (Quick Draft, Fleeting Notes) or an Android home-screen widget that writes to daily notes.
quote: Mobile is where Obsidian gets its most consistent criticism on Reddit, with iOS and Android apps working but feeling less polished than the desktop version.

claim: PDF++ turns Obsidian into an Obsidian-native PDF annotator: highlights are stored as Markdown backlinks (not embedded in the PDF), color-coded with `&color=NAME`, surfaced in the backlinks pane, and survive even if the plugin disappears.
quote: you can annotate PDF files with highlights just by linking to text selection... The value of the markdown format does not lie in the file extension!

claim: Paper Clipper adds a custom URI scheme for importing academic PDFs into the vault with structured metadata, complementing PDF++ for a Zotero-lite paper capture workflow inside Obsidian.
quote: Paper Clipper - Obsidian Plugins that Capture and manage academic papers.

claim: The Obsidian Terminal community plugin (xterm.js-based) embeds shells inside the editor, captures ANSI-colored output, exports full sessions as Markdown files, and supports regex find-in-terminal for command-output triage.
quote: exporting complete terminal sessions as markdown files, with automatic conversion of ANSI color codes... regex-capable searches through thousands of lines of output instantly.

claim: The Console Markdown plugin renders fenced `console` code blocks as styled command + output blocks, giving a low-friction way to paste shell commands and their results into a note without a live terminal.
quote: An Obsidian plugin which renders console commands and their output.

claim: SnippetBase indexes every fenced code block across the vault, supports filter-by-folder/language, free-text search, favorites, and copy-to-clipboard — converting daily notes into a searchable code snippet library.
quote: indexes fenced code snippets within a vault... filter by folder and language... search with free text... Mark snippets as favorites for quick access.

claim: Logstravaganza proxies `console.*()` calls and writes log output plus uncaught exceptions and promise rejections to a vault file in NDJSON, Markdown table, or code-block format — the standard recipe for capturing error logs from Obsidian itself.
quote: intercepts and writes all `console.*()` output to a file... captures uncaught exceptions, including those in asynchronous code and promises.

claim: A common screenshot-capture pattern uses the OS shortcut (Cmd+Ctrl+Shift+4 on Mac, Win+Shift+S on Windows) to grab the image, paste it into a Fleeting Notes browser-extension note with annotations underneath, and auto-sync into Obsidian.
quote: Use Command + Control + Shift + 4 (Mac) or Windows + Shift + S (Windows)... Click the Fleeting Notes button and add a title... Paste the captured image into the note... Add any annotations you want at the bottom of the note.

claim: The Copy Image plugin closes the loop in the other direction: right-click any image already in a note to copy it back to the system clipboard, useful for re-pasting screenshots into tickets or chats.
quote: Easily copy image to clipboard by right clicking image.

claim: Forum and r/PKMS community consensus emphasizes 'capture first, organize later' — trying to categorize ideas at capture time kills creativity, so the daily note acts as universal inbox and triage happens during a scheduled (often weekly) review.
quote: capturing first and organizing later... trying to categorize an idea the moment it strikes kills creativity.

claim: The 2026 Obsidian Web Clipper update added YouTube transcript capture and an improved Reader Mode that strips boilerplate; r/ObsidianMD users build templates auto-injecting video length, channel, and publication date into clipped notes.
quote: create templates that automatically add metadata—video length, channel name, publication date—when clipping transcripts.

claim: Reviewers in 2026 recommend pairing the Web Clipper with downstream plugins (Dataview, Linter, Auto Note Mover) so clipped notes land in an inbox folder, get cleaned up, and surface in dashboards rather than rotting unread.
quote: 7 Obsidian Plugins That Make Web Clipping Actually Useful (2026).

claim: Matuschak defines atomicity as one-concept-per-note that still captures the entirety of that concept, citing the Zettelkasten principle of separating concerns.
quote: It's best to create notes which are only about one thing—but which, as much as possible, capture the entirety of that thing. The underlying principle I'd call the principle of atomicity: put things which belong together in a Zettel, but try to separate concerns from one another.

claim: Matuschak argues notes should be factored by concept rather than by author, book, event, project, or topic so synthesis pressure and surprising cross-domain links emerge.
quote: It's best to factor Evergreen notes by concept (rather than by author, book, event, project, topic, etc). Novel connections tend to appear where they're not quite so expected. When arranging notes by concept, you may make surprising links between ideas that came up in very different books.

claim: Nick Milo's current LYT incarnation is Ideaverse Pro, which packages the ACE folder system (Atlas/Calendar/Efforts) plus the ARC ideation workflow (Add, Relate, Communicate) as the successor to the LYT Kit.
quote: Ideaverse Pro introduces the ARC framework – Add, Relate, and Communicate, which is a core ideation framework that guides ideas from conception to maturity.

claim: ACE separates a vault into three top-level folders — Atlas (knowledge), Calendar (time), Efforts (actions) — with a + inbox and X metadata folder, displacing topical folder trees.
quote: The ACE-x framework organizes a PKM system in folders for separate spaces for Atlas (knowledge), Calendar (time), and Efforts (actions), with a + (Plus) folder serving as an Inbox for new notes, and X folder for metadata.

claim: MOCs beat folders on four axes: a note can be linked from multiple MOCs, MOCs carry annotated commentary, restructuring is just editing wikilinks, and MOCs appear as nodes in graph view.
quote: A note can only live in one folder, but it can be linked from multiple MOCs simultaneously. Folders don't participate in Graph View. MOCs are real nodes in the knowledge network.

claim: A widely-cited forum heuristic splits the decision: folders for project/output workflows, links for PKM, tags treated skeptically (the OP rejects them in favor of properties).
quote: Use a lot of folders if you're using Obsidian for managing writing projects, such as articles, blog posts, video scripts, etc. Use minimal folders if you're using Obsidian for building a personal knowledge management system.

claim: Eleanor Konik defends folders for cross-tool/automation backwards-compatibility and reserves tags for verb-based action metadata like #FollowUp, #addmoc, #articleseed.
quote: Folders have good backwards compatibility. Trying to use tags for everything makes tags less useful in my opinion, because then your tags get cluttered.

claim: The tags-as-status camp uses tags to qualify processing state (#unread, #idle, #processing, #shaped, #🌱 seedling, #🌲 evergreen) so the tag mutates without breaking the link graph.
quote: Status tags are useful to qualify your notes and keep track of your progress. By using tags for status, the tag can be easily changed as the status progresses, without impacting the connections of the note.

claim: The recommended hybrid splits tags (top-down 'is-a' classifiers, status, type) from topic notes/MOCs (bottom-up emergent hubs) and warns to wait for content density before promoting clusters into MOCs.
quote: You can think of tags as indicating 'is a' category, such as 'is a' book, article, task, etc. You can think of topics as a way to facilitate connections between notes. You have to create the content before you can map it.

claim: Past ~2k notes, manual MOC maintenance breaks down; the AutoMOC plugin auto-imports backlinks, tagged mentions, and aliases into the open MOC, and Backlink Cache speeds the Backlinks pane.
quote: The AutoMOC plugin streamlines managing MOC notes by ensuring that backlinks, tagged mentions, and alias references are automatically imported into the current note. The Backlink Cache plugin significantly speeds up the performance of the Backlinks Pane for large vaults.

claim: Bases is a no-code visual database core feature shipped in Obsidian 1.9.10 that already replaces Dataview for the majority of users, with faster query speed and inline-editable cells that write back to YAML frontmatter; current limitation is table-only views.
quote: Unlike Dataview and Datacore, Bases don't require any coding—you can create and edit Bases entirely with a visual editor... Bases currently only support table views and they can only query data that is stored in properties.

claim: Power users report replacing every Dataview query with Bases tables in 2026.
quote: Users have replaced every single Dataview query with Obsidian Bases, with all the tables they used to roll up notes based on specific queries now replaced.

claim: Templater remains actively maintained at v2.20.0 (April 29 2026) with ~4.9k GitHub stars and is the JavaScript-capable templating engine for Obsidian.
quote: GitHub Star Count: 4,900 stars... latest being version 2.20.0 (April 29, 2026)... 'a template plugin for obsidian' that enables users to 'insert variables and functions results into your notes'

claim: Excalidraw plugin sits at ~6.8k GitHub stars (v2.22.1, April 2026) with monthly releases, supporting embedded sketches, LaTeX, OCR and a script-engine API.
quote: Stars: 6.8k... Latest Version: 2.22.1 (Released April 26, 2026)... Script engine for automation (ExcalidrawAutomate)

claim: Obsidian Linter (~1.9k stars, v1.31.2 March 2026) auto-formats notes via 80+ rules covering YAML, headings, footnotes and spacing.
quote: GitHub Stars: 1.9k... 80+ customizable rules organized into categories like YAML formatting, heading styles, footnote management... latest version 1.31.2 from March 2026

claim: DB Folder was archived by its owner on July 28 2025 with last release 3.5.1 (Jan 2024); users should treat it as legacy and migrate to Bases or Datacore.
quote: The repository was archived by the owner on July 28, 2025, and is now read-only... The last release (3.5.1) was published on January 19, 2024

claim: Marcusolsson's Obsidian Projects plugin was archived July 18 2025 after the author left Obsidian; a community fork (obsmd-projects) has resurrected it.
quote: The repository was archived by the owner on July 18, 2025, making it read-only. As of May 2025, Marcus Olsson decided to discontinue the plugin... someone has since reanimated the Projects plugin

claim: Smart Connections (~4.9k stars) provides local-by-default semantic note linking and chat with no API key required, with optional access to 100+ models.
quote: Smart Connections has 4.9k stars on GitHub... Operates locally by default with a built-in embedding model—no API keys required... Supports 100+ AI models via APIs

claim: logancyang/obsidian-copilot (~6.8k stars) offers ChatGPT-style vault chat plus an agentic Plus tier with web/YouTube tools, PDF/EPUB ingestion and side-by-side diff editing.
quote: GitHub Stars: 6.8k stars... Agent mode that automatically calls relevant tools (vault search, web search, YouTube)... PDF, EPUB, and image processing

claim: Obsidian Web Clipper (~4.1k stars, v1.6.2 April 2026) is the official browser extension capturing pages to Markdown with template logic, highlighting, and 2026 additions for YouTube transcripts and improved Reader Mode.
quote: GitHub Stars: 4.1k... 'Template logic (if/for)' and variable support... latest version at 1.6.2 (April 2026)

claim: Foam (VS Code, ~17.1k stars, v0.38.0 April 2026) gives developers a Git-native PKM with wiki-links, graph view, daily notes and orphan detection — strongest fit when notes already live next to source.
quote: GitHub Stars: 17.1k... Latest Release: v0.38.0 (April 16, 2026)... Graph visualization to display note connections, Wiki-style link autocompletion... Orphan and placeholder detection

claim: For developers wanting CLI integration, large-vault performance and Git-friendly Markdown, Obsidian wins over Logseq; Logseq's outliner suits rapid block capture but its flat structure can feel chaotic for reference material.
quote: Developers who live in the terminal and want automation hooks, CLI integration, large vault performance, and a portable Markdown setup that plays well with Git should go with Obsidian.

claim: SilverBullet (~5.2k stars, v2.6.1 April 2026) is a self-hosted PWA Markdown PKM with Lua scripting, an Objects/Queries database layer and offline-first sync — suits homelab/self-host users.
quote: SilverBullet has 5.2k stars on GitHub... 'a Programmable, Private, Browser-based, Open Source, Self Hosted, Personal Knowledge Management Platform'... latest release is version 2.6.1 (April 11, 2026)

claim: Tana's supertag system imposes typed schemas (fields, dates, references) on outline nodes, turning notes into queryable objects (Project/Person/Task) — strongest fit when structure-first metadata matters more than file portability.
quote: Tana's supertag system... allowing users to define structured fields — text, dates, dropdowns, references to other nodes — and apply them to any outline node. Supertags turn your notes into objects like a Project, Person, Task, Book, Recipe

claim: Reflect is a paid-only ($10/mo) networked-notes app with calendar-bound meeting notes and selectable GPT-4o/Claude Sonnet backends — narrower than Tana for technical PKM but lighter to onboard.
quote: Reflect connects notes directly to your calendar with meeting notes automatically associated with calendar events, and offers GPT-4o and Claude Sonnet as user-selectable AI backends, positioned as a paid-only service at $10/month.

claim: Obsidian's 2026 release of an official CLI extends its developer appeal for scripting and external-tool integration, widening the gap with Logseq for technical workflows.
quote: The 2026 introduction of an Obsidian CLI further extends its appeal to developers who want to automate workflows or integrate their vault into scripts and external tools.

claim: A common Obsidian maintenance pattern uses the Periodic Notes plugin to layer daily/weekly/monthly/quarterly/yearly review templates that roll up into one another.
quote: My workflow for periodic (weekly, monthly, quarterly, yearly) reviews in Obsidian

claim: Dataview queries are widely used inside weekly review notes to surface fleeting notes, open tasks, and orphaned material for the upcoming review pass.
quote: Daily and Weekly Reviews + dataview

claim: The Find Orphaned Files and Broken Links plugin emits a markdown report listing notes with no backlinks plus links pointing to non-existent files, supporting routine vault hygiene.
quote: Find orphaned files and broken links - Find files, which are nowhere linked, so they are maybe lost in your vault.

claim: The dedicated Broken Links plugin offers folder, file, and consolidated link views to triage internal links that point to missing files, headings, or blocks.
quote: identify and manage internal links within their vault that point to non-existent files, headings, or blocks

claim: The Nuke Orphans plugin trashes orphaned attachments via a single command with a confirmation prompt, automating the destructive step of attachment hygiene.
quote: Obsidian notes plugin that trashes orphaned files and attachments

claim: Andy Matuschak's evergreen-note discipline frames notes as atomic, concept-oriented units that are progressively rewritten and densely linked over time, which is the core refactor practice digital gardeners adopt.
quote: Evergreen notes are written and organized to evolve, contribute, and accumulate over time, across projects.

claim: Maggie Appleton describes growing evergreens through progressive summarisation - re-reading, bolding, splitting, and re-linking notes as the maintenance loop.
quote: as you read and re-read notes, edit and improve them through bolding, reorganizing text, adding links to other notes and external resources, and decomposing notes into smaller ones

claim: Quartz v4 is a free static-site generator that ships Obsidian compatibility, full-text search, graph view, wikilinks, transclusions, backlinks, LaTeX, syntax highlighting, popover previews, and i18n out of the box.
quote: Obsidian compatibility, full-text search, graph view, wikilinks, transclusions, backlinks, Latex, syntax highlighting, popover previews, Docker Support, internationalization, comments and many more

claim: Obsidian Publish costs $8/month annual or $10/month, while Quartz, MkDocs, Hugo/Jekyll/Eleventy and the Enveloppe/Share Note plugins are free, and Flowershow sits at $50/yr; Quartz initial setup runs roughly 30-60 minutes.
quote: $8/month annual, $10/month monthly... Initial setup takes 30-60 minutes for command-line users.

claim: A working Quartz publishing pattern keeps the vault as the source of truth, gates publication with frontmatter publish: true via Quartz's ExplicitPublish filter, and protects attachments by requiring a public- prefix.
quote: ExplicitPublish(), [which] only forwards Markdown pages where the frontmatter has `publish: true` set.

claim: Material for MkDocs positions itself as an extensible open-source documentation theme aimed at making knowledge-sharing easier and more polished, suitable when a vault is more documentation than garden.
quote: Material for MkDocs is described as an extensible, Open Source tool that makes sharing knowledge both easier and beautiful for writers and learners.

claim: Astro Starlight provides a ready-made docs theme on top of Astro that is fast, accessible, and easy to maintain, an attractive option for JS-leaning gardeners migrating from MkDocs.
quote: Astro Starlight is a documentation theme built on the Astro web framework that provides a ready-made structure for creating documentation websites that are fast, accessible, and easy to maintain.

claim: Maggie Appleton's garden remains a canonical public example: visual essays on programming, design and anthropology arranged non-linearly with categories, tags, and curated imagery rather than chronological feed.
quote: filled with visual essays on programming, design, and anthropology... non-linear structure

claim: Joel Hooks' garden is frequently cited as a tone-setting example that explicitly rejects the blog frame in favour of perpetually-edited notes.
quote: My blog is a digital garden, not a blog

claim: The best-of-digital-gardens list maintains a ranked inventory of public second-brain sites that gardeners use as benchmarks for layout, linking density, and update cadence.
quote: Ranked list of awesome digital gardens / second brains
